rationale: Within the citing paper, Melumad et al. (2019) is used to support concrete premises in the argument, including that smartphone users produce more emotional language and that smartphone constraints can lead to shorter reviews. These claims are used to motivate the paper's central question of whether smartphones also change what consumers choose. The paper later positions its contribution against prior smartphone research on content generation, indicating a meaningful continuation rather than a purely perfunctory mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends the line of work represented by Melumad et al. (2019) from smartphone effects on user-generated content to smartphone effects on choice outcomes. It keeps the broader device-effects framing but advances a different focal consequence and mechanism, emphasizing private self-focus and uniqueness preference.
